title: Fill a Page: When the Counter Wraps
lesson:
The 6502's registers are 8 bits, so they count 0 to 255 and then wrap back to 0. That's not a bug to avoid, it's a tool. A 'page' is 256 bytes ($00 to $FF), and the screen is exactly four pages tall. The top quarter of the screen is one full page: $0200 to $02FF.

Here's the neat trick: load X with 0, store, INX, and BNE, with no CPX at all. After 255, INX wraps X back to 0, which sets the Zero flag, and BNE falls through. One loop, exactly 256 passes, no comparison needed. Early programmers squeezed cycles like this constantly, because on a 1 MHz chip, every instruction counted.
prompt: Fill the top quarter of the screen, the whole 256-byte page from $0200 to $02FF, with a single non-black colour. Use the counter-wrap trick (no CPX needed).
starterCode:
; Fill one 256-byte page. Let X wrap from 255 back to 0.
LDX #$00
LDA #$05       ; green
loop:
  STA $0200,X
  INX
  BNE loop     ; loops until X wraps back to 0
BRK
hints: INX after 255 wraps X to 0 and sets the Zero flag, so BNE then exits.
No CPX is needed: STA $0200,X then INX then BNE loop runs exactly 256 times.
successText: All 256 bytes from $0200 to $02FF must be painted.
reference:
LDX #$00
LDA #$05
loop:
  STA $0200,X
  INX
  BNE loop
BRK
